title: Adiabatic Quantum Simulation of the Topological Su--Schrieffer--Heeger--Hubbard Model
authors: Ssu-Yi Chen, Bo-Hung Chen, Dah-Wei Chiou, Jie-Hong Roland Jiang
abstract: We develop an adiabatic quantum simulation framework on gate-based quantum computers to probe topological signatures of the one-dimensional fermionic Su--Schrieffer--Heeger--Hubbard (SSHH) model. We present explicit quantum-circuit constructions for initial-state preparation and time evolution, together with a practical measurement protocol and classical post-processing procedure for extracting the many-body Berry phase and the spatial profile of the sublattice polarization. Using classical simulations of the proposed circuits, we demonstrate -- for the first time within a genuine many-body framework -- that the topological characteristics of the SSH model remain robust against weak Hubbard interactions but eventually break down as the chiral-symmetry-breaking component of the interaction exceeds a threshold. The required qubit number, gate complexity, measurement shots, and classical pre- and post-processing costs all scale polynomially with system size. Our results provide a proof-of-concept framework for probing topological properties of interacting many-body systems via adiabatic quantum simulation on future large-scale quantum computers.
summary: The authors present a framework for simulating the SSHH model on gate-based quantum computers using adiabatic evolution. They show that topological features like the Berry phase are preserved under weak electron-electron interactions but vanish when symmetry-breaking interactions become too strong. This work serves as a proof-of-concept for using quantum computers to study the stability of topological phases in interacting systems.
main_problem: Developing a scalable gate-based quantum computing framework to probe the topological signatures and robustness of the many-body Su-Schrieffer-Heeger-Hubbard (SSHH) model.
main_result: Demonstrated that topological characteristics like the many-body Berry phase and edge-state polarization are robust against weak Hubbard interactions but break down when chiral-symmetry-breaking interactions exceed a specific threshold.
method: Adiabatic quantum simulation using a first-order Trotter decomposition on a gate-based quantum computer, including explicit circuit constructions for state preparation and time evolution.
model_or_system: The one-dimensional fermionic Su-Schrieffer-Heeger-Hubbard (SSHH) model, which includes topological hopping (intra-cell v and inter-cell w) and on-site Hubbard interactions (U_A and U_B).
key_observables: Many-body Berry phase, spatial profile of sublattice polarization, charge center, and edge-occupation operator.
important_parameters: Hopping amplitudes (v, w), Hubbard interaction strengths (U, delta U), evolution time (T), and number of Trotter intervals (L).
main_assumptions: The adiabatic approximation assumes a non-degenerate path and a sufficiently large evolution time; the framework relies on the interaction being weak enough to avoid closing the energy gap.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the quantum circuit for a Trotter step; Fig 2 shows Trotter convergence fidelity; Fig 3 shows the many-body Berry phase as a function of intercell hopping; Fig 4 shows the spatial profile of sublattice polarization.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of probing many-body topology, describes the SSHH model and the adiabatic simulation framework, details the quantum circuit construction and measurement protocols, presents numerical results demonstrating topological robustness and breakdown, and concludes with the scalability of the approach.
why_interesting: It provides a practical, polynomially scaling blueprint for using near-term quantum hardware to study complex topological properties in interacting many-body systems that are difficult to capture with standard variational methods.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Quantum circuit implementation of a single Trotter step for the model with N = 2 unit cells. Qubits q0 to q3 and q4 to q7 encode the spin-up and spin-down orbitals, respectively. The circuit is partitioned into three main stages: (1) The red shaded part represents the intra-cell hopping operations, utilizing R and G gates to realizing the real and imaginary components. (2) The blue shaded part represents the inter-cell hopping operations. The R and G gates in the gray boxes represent the periodic-boundary hopping terms, present only in PBC. (3) The green shaded part represents the Hubbard interaction, implemented via Controlled-Phase gates, which entangle the corresponding spin-up...
Fig. 2: Final-state fidelity for the case with U = 0 (top) and the case with U = 1 (bottom).
Fig. 3: Many-body Berry phase for different values of ∆U.
Fig. 4: Sublattice polarization for different values of ∆U.
